coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent enhancement of polyethyleneimine nano-polymers and the application in cellar imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lei
Raohao, Fang
Changchang, Zhang
Xiang, Liu
Changhua, Zhang
coredata.dc:description: Nano-polymers (NPs) with non-conjugated moiety emit bright fluorescence when irradiated by ultraviolet lights, which arouses extensive interests since they do not have traditional fluorophores or conjugated moieties. Although the mechanism was put forward in some reports and entitled as “crosslink enhanced emission” (CEE) the oxidation of starting material is also contributable for the enhanced fluorescence according to our researches. However this has been neglected until now. Herein, when aqueous solution of pure polyethyleneimine (PEI) was suffered from heating at 180° in air for 60 min crosslink of PEI molecules took place and produced PEI NPs. It emitted bright blue fluorescence and the quantum yield reached as high as 18.24% while PEI itself possessed none of fluorescence. Infrared, 1H nuclear magnetic resonance and X-ray photoelectron spectra disclosed the oxidation of PEI during the lasting heating. And the image of transmission electron microscope confirmed the production of PEI NPs. When PEI NPs were prepared in the presence of hydrogen peroxide they performed a successive enhancement of fluorescent intensity, which demonstrated the oxidation of PEI contributed a lot for the fluorescent enhancement of PEI NPs. Experimental results also demonstrated that the PEI NPs have a potential application in cellar imaging on account of the lower cytotoxicity and the reactivity to the activated folic acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-conjugated nano-polymer
Polyethyleneimine
Oxidation
Aggregation
Cellar imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Heating treatment on the aqueous solution of PEI
Covalent linkage between PEI NPs and folic acid (FA) and cellar viability assay
Enhanced fluorescence of PEI NPs and the oxidation of PEI
Cellular cytotoxicity of PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of PEI and PEI NPs under varied excitation wavelength; (b) UV-vis spectra of PEI and PEI NPs; (c) Fluorescence spectra of aqueous solutions of PEI suffered from heating under varied temperature for 60 min (38.0 mg mL−1).

Regressed lines between integrated fluorescent intensity and the absorbance of the sample obtained from heating for 60 min. (a) Reflux-heating at 100 °C, (b) oven-heating at 100 °C, (c) oven-heating at 180 °C. The slopes of the lines are also displayed in the figure.
Comparisons of IR and 1H NMR spectra between PEI NPs and PEI.
XPS of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of PEI and of PEI NPs corresponding to (a ∼ c) and (d ∼ f) respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PEI solution suffered from reflux-heating for 2 h and 4 h at the absent of or in the present of hydroquinone; (b) Fluorescent spectra of PEI solution suffered from oven-heating at the absent of or in the present of H2O2; (c) Fluorescent spectra of PEI solution disposed with the similar way at the absent of or in the present of hydroquinone. (Excitation wavelength = 340 nm).
TEM images of PEI NPs with different scale.
Variation of fluorescent intensity of aqueous solution of PEI NPs with the pH value. (The initial concentration of PEI NPs is 6 × 10−3 mg mL−1 and the pH equals 8.0).
Cellular viabilities of HeLa cells at varied dose of PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA respectively.
Confocal fluorescence microscope images of HeLa cells incubated with PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA for 0 and 8 h respectively under bright field and 488 nm excitation.
XPS information of full width at half maxima (FWHM) and atomic percent of PEI and PEI NPs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent enhancement of polyethyleneimine nano-polymers and the application in cellar imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano-polymers (NPs) with non-conjugated moiety emit bright fluorescence when irradiated by ultraviolet lights, which arouses extensive interests since they do not have traditional fluorophores or conjugated moieties. Although the mechanism was put forward in some reports and entitled as “crosslink enhanced emission” (CEE) the oxidation of starting material is also contributable for the enhanced fluorescence according to our researches. However this has been neglected until now. Herein, when aqueous solution of pure polyethyleneimine (PEI) was suffered from heating at 180° in air for 60 min crosslink of PEI molecules took place and produced PEI NPs. It emitted bright blue fluorescence and the quantum yield reached as high as 18.24% while PEI itself possessed none of fluorescence. Infrared, 1H nuclear magnetic resonance and X-ray photoelectron spectra disclosed the oxidation of PEI during the lasting heating. And the image of transmission electron microscope confirmed the production of PEI NPs. When PEI NPs were prepared in the presence of hydrogen peroxide they performed a successive enhancement of fluorescent intensity, which demonstrated the oxidation of PEI contributed a lot for the fluorescent enhancement of PEI NPs. Experimental results also demonstrated that the PEI NPs have a potential application in cellar imaging on account of the lower cytotoxicity and the reactivity to the activated folic acid.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-conjugated nano-polymers arouse massive interests since they do not have conventional fluorophores or conjugated moieties while they emit bright fluorescence. Several organic polymers [1–4], such as cocoon, poly (vinyl alcohol), hyper-branched or linear polyethyleneimine, could be cross-linked under certain conditions forming nano-polymers (NPs) with intensive fluorescence. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) molecules were cross-linked in the presence of carbon tetrachloride under hydrothermal treatment at 90° for 6 h generating nano-polymers, which performed an enhanced fluorescence compared with PEI solution. The fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of the nano-polymers reached 3.06% [5]. Rapid combination between hexahydro-1,3,5-tris(hy-droxyethyl)-s-triazine and dicyclohexylmethane 4,4′-diisocyanate via the esterification of hydroxyl and isocyanate also generated NPs [6], in which the triazine with branched chains were linked covalently. The QY reached 1.1–2.2% in mixed solvent. Hydrothermal treatment of poly (vinyl alcohol) caused crosslink of vinyl chains and the production of NPs with a degree of crystallinity via intermolecular dehydration, which emitted bright fluorescence at 475 nm [3]. The fluorescent QY reached 1.26%. Chains of polyacrylic acid could be cross-linked by the ethylenediamine though reaction of the carboxyl and primary amines [7]. And the QY reached as high as 44.18%. Monomers of N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidone with weak fluorescence were polymerized producing poly (N-vinylpyrrolidone), which gave strong fluorescence [8]. Yang group discussed the mechanism of the enhanced fluorescence in detail and entitled this as crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) [9,10]. The crosslink increased molecular rigidity hence the resulted NPs exhibited significantly enhanced fluorescence. In their researches, macromolecules with hyper-branched chains or polymers with repeated non-conjugated structural units were often chosen as starting materials. When their skeletons were cross-linked covalently the fluorescence was enhanced a lot. Actually, the sub-fluorophores in the molecules, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino groups, etc., played the vital role in the enhanced emission. Firstly, chains of the macromolecules could be joined together depending on the reaction among these functional groups and the added crosslink agents. For example, ethylenediamine joined the chains of polyacrylic acid via the reaction between amino group and carboxyl [7]. Carbon tetrachloride played the similar role in joining PEI by replacement of chlorine with the amino group [2,5]. That enhanced the rigidity of molecular skeleton, which reduced energetic loss when excited electrons transmitted back to ground state according to the CEE theory. Secondly, the sub-fluorophores might be involved in the radiative transition and contributed a lot for the fluorescence since the p orbital of sub-fluorophores was full of electrons. These orbitals might participate in regional conjugation via p-π interaction.

It is worth mentioning that some of these sub-fluorophores such as amino group is apt to be oxidized by ambient oxygen or added oxidizer such as ammonium persulfate [11]. It is very possible that the oxidation induces significant enhancement of fluorescence [8,12]. Although such a crosslink among the chains strengthened the rigidity of the NPs which was advantageous for the enhanced fluorescence the oxidization of amino-contained compounds [8] is unavoidable that may also be contributable for the fluorescence under the synthesizing conditions. Amino group is inclined to be oxidized in natural environment, as is well known to all. However, this was neglected in several previous reports when the fluorescent mechanism was tried to be explained [13–15]. Herein, we will report a convenient way of producing PEI NPs without an addition of any crosslink agent and demonstrate the oxidation of PEI was the main factor for the enhanced fluorescence.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight 600, 99%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30% in water), ethylenediamine (≥99.5%), 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine (99%) and N,N,N′,N'-tetramethylethylenediamine (99%), folic acid (FA, ≥98%), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 98.0%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), quinine sulfate dehydrate (98%) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China) and without any purification before use. Double-distilled water was used thought out all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heating treatment on the aqueous solution of PEI
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solution of 5 mL PEI (38.0 mg mL−1) was added to a stainless steel plate which was placed in an air-drying oven with forced convection at 180° for 60 min. The water component was evaporated quickly and the PEI NPs were generated on bottle of the vessel. Fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible spectra of PEI NPs were measured respectively and had a comparison with those of PEI solution. Such an experiment was repeated at a varied temperature for inquiring the effect of heating temperature on fluorescent intensity of the PEI NPs.

Similarly, 5 mL PEI solutions (38.0 mg mL−1) in a round-bottom flask was treated with reflux-heating at 100 °C for 60 min. During the reflux-heating, water could not be removed from the round-bottom flask while the oven-heating resulted in the loss of water that permitted more PEI molecules contacted with environmental oxygen. The fluorescent QY were measured to demonstrate the effect of oxidation on the fluorescent feature of PEI.

Additionally, reflux-heating on PEI solution in the presence of 1.0 mg hydroquinone at 100 °C for 2 and 4 h as well as oven-heating at 180 °C for 60 min on PEI solution mixed with 1.0 mL H2O2 and 1.0 mg hydroquinone respectively were carried out for inquiring the relationship between oxidation of PEI and fluorescent intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent linkage between PEI NPs and folic acid (FA) and cellar viability assay
ce:section.ce:para: Weighed 4.7 mg EDC (0.0245 mmol) and 3.1 mg folic acid (0.0070 mmol) and put them into 20 mL water. Such a solution was stirred at room temperature for 2 h. Then 3.2 mg NHS (0.0278 mmol) were put in and kept the reaction for another 2 h for introducing the active NHS ester onto FA. At the end of the reaction, 20 mg PEI NPs were added in for preparing FA-functionalized PEI NPs (PEI NPs-FA) and the reaction lasted for another 6 h. Finally, the mixture was loaded into a dialysis bag (MW 3000) for 24 h and refreshed the outside water every 8 h to remove any impurity.

The cytotoxicity of the PEI NPs was examined with MTT assay. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates and were cultured for overnight in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). After treatments with PEI NPs-FA at varied concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μg mL−1) for 48 h cultivation, 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide solution was added to every cell well for additional 4 h incubation. The culture medium was discarded and dimethyl sulfoxide was added to dissolve the formazan blue, which was formed in the cells. The absorbance at 490 nm was recorded with a Thermo Scientific Multiskan spectrum microplate spectrophotometer. The cellular viability was estimated according to the following equation.
Cellular Viability (%) = Atreated/Acontrol × 100%
Where Acontrol was obtained in the absence of PEI NPs and Atreated was obtained in the presence of PEI NPs.

The effects of heating temperature and ambient oxygen on the fluorescent intensity of PEI were revealed with fluorescence spectra (LS 45, PerkinElmer). The fluorescent QY of samples were measured by comparison with the solution of quinine sulfate in 0.5 mol L−1 sulfuric acid (55.00%) which used as a standard substance. The fluorescent enhancement of PEI NPs was estimated by the augment of fluorescent intensity or fluorescent QY. The oxidation of PEI was demonstrated by infrared (IR, Nicolet 380, Thermo Nicolet Corporation) and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR, BRUKER 300, TMS as an internal standard) as well as X-ray photo-electron energy spectra (XPS, KRATOS AMICUS). Spectra of N 1s and O 1s were recorded with a high-resolution to demonstrate the oxidation of amino groups in PEI. The spectra were deconvoluted in nonlinear least-square fitting to analyze the changes of binding energy of elemental N and O. Images of transition electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company) confirmed the nano sizes of PEI NPs. Photographs of fluorescent confocal microscope were taken on Fluoview FV1000 (Olympus).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced fluorescence of PEI NPs and the oxidation of PEI
ce:section.ce:para: When PEI solution is suffered from the lasting heating at 180 °C for 60 min the fluorescent intensity of the product (PEI NPs) increases extraordinarily, revealed in Fig. 1 (a). Maximum of the intensity corresponds to the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. However, aqueous solution of PEI performs rather weak fluorescence and it's hard to be detected. UV-vis of Fig. 1(b) also exhibits the obvious changes. Aqueous solution of PEI has no absorbance from 280 nm to visible region while PEI NPs have a distinct absorbing at 340 nm. The fluorescent intensity of aqueous solution of PEI varies with the heating temperature, which exhibited in Fig. 1(c). The enhanced temperature induces more intensive fluorescence. The fluorescent intensity of 180 °C is far greater than that of 60 °C.

Presumably, the photoluminescence of PEI NPs should be attributed to the new occurred ultraviolet absorbing. Why the fluorescent intensity jumps so much only when the aqueous solution of PEI is subjected to the lasting heating in atmosphere? What is the main cause for this giant alteration? Such a facile operation arouse obvious enhancement of fluorescence which evokes our interests for seeking the real reasons behind these experimental facts.

Additionally, when aqueous solutions of PEI (10 mL, 38.0 mg mL−1) are suffered from reflux-heating and oven-heating respectively at 100 °C for 60 min the fluorescent feature of the both solutions arise. The integrated fluorescent intensity dependent with the absorbance is disclosed in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) respectively. The slopes of regressed lines between integrated fluorescent intensity and the absorbance are used to calculate the fluorescent QY, which are measured by comparison them with standard substance of quinine sulfate in 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 (describe in Supporting Information). The QY of sample with oven-heating at 100 °C reaches 5.67% while the one with reflux-heating arrives 2.02%. If the aqueous PEI solution is heated in oven at 180 °C for 60 min the slope of regressed line increases a lot as is seen in Fig. 2(c); and the QY arrives as high as 18.24%.

Why does the facile treatment arouse the jump of the QY? What is the real reason for this phenomenon? The experiments are executed in the absence of any chemical reagent except the ambient oxygen. It is possible that surrounding oxygen is related with the distinct enhancement of the fluorescent intensity [8]. That is to say oxidation of PEI may occur during the production of PEI NPs. Obviously, the oxidation in apt to take place with an increased temperature.

When aqueous PEI solution is reflux heated the water component is always remained in the solution. The concentration of PEI keeps unvaried. On the contrary, oven-heating permits escape of water that concentration of PEI is improved gradually during the heating. In this case, more PEI molecules contact the ambient oxygen and are oxidized by oxygen. Therefore the fluorescent QY reaches 5.67% and is greater than 2.02% obtained in reflux-heating. The QY of 5.67% is also greater than 3.65% of PEI NPs obtained in hydrothermal synthesis reported in previous reference [2]. During the hydrothermal treatment described in this reference, water of PEI solution can not escape from the reactor. The high pressure improves the boiling point of water, which brings an intensive oxidation of PEI. The authors attribute the enhanced QY to the crosslink of PEI in presence of carbon tetrachloride. However, the authors do not provide the chlorine information in the X-ray photoelectron spectra. Hence we doubt whether the carbon tetrachloride involve in the crosslink reaction. In our opinions, the enhanced QY described in the report should be attributed mainly to the oxidization of PEI since it is almost equal with that of reflux-heating herein.

The oxidation of PEI is also proved by IR and 1H NMR spectra, which revealed in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3(a), bands 3347 and 3286 cm−1 are arisen from symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibration of –NH2. Peaks of 1578 and 1464 cm−1 should be attributed to scissoring vibration of –NH2 and –NH– respectively. IR spectrum of PEI NPs changes greatly when compared with that of PEI. Primary amino groups remain in PEI NPs, which judged from stretching vibration of –NH2 in 3432 cm−1. However, the shifting to a high frequency, along with the scissoring vibration at 1662 cm−1, suggests the oxidation of amino groups. The oxidation of primary amino groups results in the blue shift of stretching vibration of N–H. 1H NMR information also demonstrates the structural changes of PEI NPs. Chemical shifts of 2.71–2.35 ppm in Fig. 3(c) should belong to C H
2NH2, C H
2NH and C H
2N of PEI. Nevertheless, such 1H signals in PEI NPs move to higher chemical shifts. This hints the oxidation of amino groups as well.

XPS measurements of PEI and PEI NPs also demonstrate oxygen involves in the production of PEI NPs. Table 1 shows that atomic percent of O reaches 17.36% which is far greater than 3.18% of PEI and the atomic percent of C and N decrease correspondingly. This supports the above analyses that the ambient oxygen participates in the chemical changes of PEI. Narrow scans on C, N and O shown in Fig. 4 (a ∼ f) also agree this declaration. Obviously, the PEI FWHM of C, N and O are less than the one of PEI NPs, as is also recorded in Table 1. Since several C and N atoms of PEI are oxidized by the surrounding oxygen during the lasting heating at 180 °C Fig. 4(d) and (e) exhibit newly occurred components of C and N. Peak of 284.8 eV should be arisen from –CH2–CH2– while 287.2 eV should be due to –CH2–O–. Bands of 398.8 [16] and 399.6 eV [17] in Fig. 4(e) should be attributed to –NH2 or –NH–CH2– and –N=O respectively. More FWHM of O 1s shown in Fig. 4(f) suggests more oxygenic component, which also suggests the involvement of ambient oxygen. Therefore, XPS measurement proves oxidation of PEI definitely during the heating treatment. That is vital for fluorescent enhancement of the PEI NPs.

Even the reflux-heating at 100 °C on PEI solution still arouse the increase of fluorescent intensity, which disclosed in Fig. 5 (a). That should be attributed to the oxidation of ambient oxygen. When aqueous solution of PEI mixed with 1.0 mL H2O2 is oven-heated at 180 °C for 60 min fluorescent intensity increase six-fold more approximately, shown in Fig. 5(b). However, color of the solution becomes deeper than the one obtained without H2O2. Thus the absorbance increases a lot too. Hence the fluorescent QY in the presence of H2O2 does not increase proportionally. The liberated oxygen of H2O2 intensifies the oxidation of PEI. When PEI solution is mixed with 1.0 mg hydroquinone and disposed with the same way the fluorescent intensity decreases more than a half, which revealed in Fig. 5(c). Hydroquinone, as an antioxidant, is applied intensively in protecting monomers for avoiding oxidation that prolongs the storage life. Obviously, hydroquinone herein prohibits the enhancement of fluorescent intensity. Likewise, this phenomenon still exists in reflux-heating on PEI solution in the presence of hydroquinone shown in Fig. 5(a). These suggest the fluorescent enhancement is close related with oxidation of amino groups.

In fact, some small molecules of amines such as ethylenediamine, 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine and N,N,N′,N'-tetramethylethylenediamine possess the similar features. When their aqueous solutions are heated to 100 °C for 60 min all the fluorescent intensities improve [18]. When such solutions are mixed with H2O2 and suffered from reflux-heating the fluorescent intensities increase successively, which displayed in Fig. S1 of Supporting Information. H2O2 intensifies the oxidation of the amino groups. Among these amines, the fluorescent intensity of N,N,N′,N'-tetramethylethylenediamine is the weakest while the one of ethylenediamine is the most intensive, which is easily observed from Fig. S1. That exclaims the substituted groups on amino group may inhibit the oxidation. When solutions of these amines are mixed with 1.0 mg of hydroquinone and reflux-heated at 100 °C for 60 min, all the fluorescent intensities are depressed since hydroquinone postpones the oxidation of these amines in this case. Such experiments demonstrate that the fluorescent intensity has something to do with the oxidation of amino groups definitely.

Summarily, oxidation of PEI should not be neglected when PEI is disposed with heating [2,5] since it is impossible that ambient oxygen is isolated completely. Contribution of oxidized PEI for the enhanced fluorescence should not be ignored when discussing the fluorescent mechanism of synthesized nano material which containing organic amino groups.

That tells us when we discuss the fluorescent mechanism of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot, prepared with carbohydrate and organic amine [13–15,19,20], the contribution of oxidized amine on the enhanced QY of carbon quantum dot should be considerate carefully. It should be checked whether organic amine alone is responsible for the enhanced fluorescence under the synthesizing conditions. Additionally, the oxidation of amine should be included in the enhanced emission when CEE of the amine-contained PDs is discussed.

TEM images (Fig. 6 ) demonstrate nano sizes of PEI NPs after the aqueous PEI solution is suffered from a lasting heating in an oven at 180 °C for 60 min. The sizes of the most aggregated particles are less than 50 nm. The generation of PEI NPs depends on the self-assembly of PEI without help of any additional chemical reagent. Such self-assembly processes rely on intensive interaction of hydrogen bond or polar interaction among the chains of PEI and physical intertwining of the chains. The aggregation of PEI complete when the water component is removed completely. In this case, it relies on the intensive interaction among the branched chains of PEI rather than crosslink agent. CEE described in some reports [2,5–7,21] concerns usually with additional crosslink agents which link the chains of precursor of NPs. For example, mixture of polyacrylic acid and ethylenediamine were treated hydrothermally generating nano NPs [7] via the combination between carboxyl and amino groups. Poly (vinyl alcohol) nano-polymers prepared in a hydro-thermal reactor [3] emits blue fluorescence. The authors ascribed these to the crosslink among molecules by virtue of the combination between hydroxyl of one chain and hydrogen of another chain. Production of PEI NPs herein should be derived from superamolecule interaction which also belongs to CEE [9,22].

The way of oven-heating favors escape of the water therefore it is helpful for the self-assembly of PEI. Actually, the CEE phenomenon herein is quite different from aggregation-induced emission (AIE) described in many reports invented by Tang group [23–25]. The AIE described in their reports is derived from the aggregation in special medium with the specific composition. The mixed media, such as mixture of water, acetone, methanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, acetonitrile, etc., result in aggregating of the target compound. Both AIE and CEE regard molecular rigidity as the main cause for the enhanced fluorescence, which decreases the energy loss when excited electrons transit back to ground state. Hence, CEE comes from chemical synthesis under intensive conditions while AIE depends on dispersing media. The CEE phenomenon of PEI herein should consider the role of oxidation of PEI. During the crosslink of PEI, oxidation by ambient oxygen is unavoidable. Moreover, PEI is more apt to be oxidized by the ambient oxygen at a higher temperature.

In addition, the pH value influences the fluorescent intensity of PEI NPs distinctly, which indicated in Fig. 7 . The intensity decreases with the increased pH value and increase with the decreased pH value, exhibited in Fig. 7(a) and (b) respectively. The fluorescent intensity at pH 6.5 is almost twice the one at pH 8.0. The sudden increase implies the fluorescent intensity of PEI NPs is sensitive extremely with acidic environment. That should be arisen from augment of molecular rigidity when protons combine with amino groups, which is beneficial for the enhancement of QY [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular cytotoxicity of PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytotoxicity at varied dose of PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA was evaluated with HeLa cells by MTT assay. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . It reveals the low cytotoxicity of PEI NPs. Even the concentration of PEI NPs reaches 50 μg mL−1 the cellular viability still approaches 100% approximately. The FA-functionalized PEI NPs (PEI NPs-FA) also display the low cytotoxicity. It is clear that both PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA are safety for HeLa cells.

Thus PEI NPs and PEI NPs-FA can be applied in targeted bioimaging, which exhibited in Fig. 9 . We can not observe fluorescence inside HeLa cells judged from (a) and (b) even the incubating time reaches 8 h. However, PEI NPs-FA penetrates the cytomembrane of HeLa cell and bright blue areas can be observed after 8 h of the culturing. This also claims FA molecules have been bonded covalently with PEI NPs. Hence PEI NPs functionalized with FA molecules have potential bio-applications in fluorescence imaging.

We provide a convenient approach of synthesizing PEI NPs with sole PEI solution without any additional chemical crosslink reagent. The experimental evidences prove that PEI molecules are oxidized during the process of producing PEI NPs. The fluorescence of PEI NPs should be derived from the oxidation and the aggregation of PEI. The oxidized amines contribute a lot for the enhanced fluorescent feature of PEI NPs, which also confirmed by the experiments in the presence of H2O2 and hydroquinone. Therefore the mechanism for the enhanced QY of nitrogen-doped carbon nano dots or carbon quantum dots should be thought over with more carefulness since it is very possible that the oxidation of organic amines occur during nano material synthesis. Actually, many reports had described the fluorescent enhancement of carbon quantum dots when doped with amines. Such enhanced fluorescence may be duo to the oxidation of starting material of amines according to our experiments [13–15,20,26–28]. Additionally, the PEI NPs with a lower cytotoxicity and intensive fluorescence have potential applications in bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province (KJ2017A047). The authors acknowledge assistances of Dr. Zi Liu during the bio-experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymdegradstab.2019.01.013.
